arguments.name: fix-csproj-and-special
arguments.prompt:
Fix AOT compatibility issues in these specific files in `/tmp/sv-5b13ff0e4fea41a3bc02c3bbb0e14bc3/before/`. Make ONLY the changes specified below. Do not make any other changes.

## 1. Azure.ResourceManager.csproj — Add IsAotCompatible

Add `<IsAotCompatible>true</IsAotCompatible>` inside the existing `<PropertyGroup>`, after the `<NoWarn>` line (line 14).

## 2. ArmEnvironment.cs (line 80) — Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(this)`

The current line 80:
```csharp
public override string ToString() => JsonSerializer.Serialize(this);
```

Replace with manual JSON building (ArmEnvironment is a simple struct with Endpoint and Audience):
```csharp
public override string ToString()
{
    using var stream = new System.IO.MemoryStream();
    using (var writer = new Utf8JsonWriter(stream))
    {
        writer.WriteStartObject();
        writer.WriteString("Endpoint"u8, Endpoint?.ToString());
        writer.WriteString("Audience"u8, Audience);
        writer.WriteString("DefaultScope"u8, DefaultScope);
        writer.WriteEndObject();
    }
    return System.Text.Encoding.UTF8.GetString(stream.ToArray());
}
```

Note: The file already has `using System.Text.Json;` imported. No additional usings are needed.

## 3. ArmClientOptions.cs (line 52) — Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<...>>(span)` 

Current line 52:
```csharp
var allProfile = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>>(span);
```

Replace with manual parsing using JsonDocument:
```csharp
using var profileDoc = JsonDocument.Parse(BinaryData.FromStream(stream).ToMemory());
var allProfile = new Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>();
foreach (var topProp in profileDoc.RootElement.EnumerateObject())
{
    var inner = new Dictionary<string, JsonElement>();
    foreach (var innerProp in topProp.Value.EnumerateObject())
    {
        inner[innerProp.Name] = innerProp.Value.Clone();
    }
    allProfile[topProp.Name] = inner;
}
```

Also remove the now-unused `span` variable on the line before (line 51). The current code is:
```csharp
var span = BinaryData.FromStream(stream).ToMemory().Span;
var allProfile = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>>(span);
```

Replace BOTH those lines with the block above. Note: the `stream` variable is already defined earlier. The BinaryData.FromStream(stream) call just moves from the span line into the new JsonDocument.Parse.

## 4. Extensions/ArmClientBuilderExtensions.cs (line 30) — Mark method with RequiresUnreferencedCode

The method at line 27-31 calls `builder.RegisterClientFactory<ArmClient, ArmClientOptions>(configuration)` which has `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]`. Add the `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` and `[RequiresDynamicCode]` attributes to the `AddArmClient<TBuilder, TConfiguration>` method. The file already has `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;`.

Add these attributes before line 27:
```csharp
[RequiresUnreferencedCode("Dependency injection may require types that cannot be statically analyzed.")]
[RequiresDynamicCode("Dependency injection may require types that cannot be statically analyzed.")]
```

## 5. ArmOperation.cs — Add DynamicallyAccessedMembers to type parameter

Lines 96-111: The `IsResource<T>()` method uses `typeof(T).GetConstructor(...)` and `Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(T), ...)`. 

Add `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)]` constraint to the `T` type parameter of:
- `IsResource<T>()` (line 96)
- `Rehydrate<T>()` (line 44) 
- `RehydrateAsync<T>()` (line 82)

For `IsResource<T>()`, the current signature is:
```csharp
private static bool IsResource<T>() where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
private static bool IsResource<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>() where T : notnull
```

For `Rehydrate<T>()` (line 44):
```csharp
public static ArmOperation<T> Rehydrate<T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
public static ArmOperation<T> Rehydrate<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```

For `RehydrateAsync<T>()` (line 82):
```csharp
public static async Task<ArmOperation<T>> RehydrateAsync<T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
public static async Task<ArmOperation<T>> RehydrateAsync<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```

Also add `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;` to the file's using directives.

## 6. GenericOperationSource.cs — Add DynamicallyAccessedMembers to type parameter

The class `GenericOperationSource<T>` (line 16) uses `ModelReaderWriter.Read(data, typeof(T))` and `Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(T), ...)`.

The `ModelReaderWriter.Read` requires `PublicConstructors | NonPublicConstructors`, and the `Activator.CreateInstance` requires `NonPublicConstructors`.

Change the class declaration from:
```csharp
internal class GenericOperationSource<T> : IOperationSource<T>
```
to:
```csharp
internal class GenericOperationSource<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.PublicConstructors | DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T> : IOperationSource<T>
```

The file already has `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;`.

arguments.name: fix-lro-files
fix-serialize-write-calls
fix-deserialize-calls
arguments.prompt:
Fix AOT compatibility in serialization files in `/tmp/sv-5b13ff0e4fea41a3bc02c3bbb0e14bc3/before/`. Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<T>(rawText)` calls with AOT-safe static Deserialize methods.

All these types have static `Deserialize*` methods that take a `JsonElement` and `ModelReaderWriterOptions`. When the pattern is `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<T>(property.Value.GetRawText())`, replace with the type's static deserialize method using `property.Value` directly (it's already a `JsonElement`).

For `ResponseError` (from Azure.Core), it implements `IJsonModel<ResponseError>`. Use: `ResponseError.DeserializeResponseError(JsonElement.ParseValue(/* reader from raw text */))` — actually, the safest approach for ResponseError is to parse the raw text and use the JsonConverter, but since we want AOT, we should use the model's own IJsonModel. The simplest AOT-safe approach for ResponseError which implements IJsonModel is:
```csharp
error = ModelReaderWriter.Read<ResponseError>(BinaryData.FromString(property.Value.GetRawText()), ModelSerializationExtensions.WireOptions, AzureResourceManagerContext.Default);
```
Wait, ResponseError is from Azure.Core and may not be in AzureResourceManagerContext. Let me suggest a simpler approach: use `JsonDocument.Parse` + the existing converter through `WriteObjectValue` pattern.

Actually, for deserialization of `ResponseError`, the AOT-safe approach is:
```csharp
error = ResponseError.DeserializeResponseError(property.Value);
```
Let me check if that method exists... If it doesn't, we need a different approach. Let's use the `IJsonModel<ResponseError>` Create method:
```csharp
// For ResponseError, let's use the fact that it has a converter and parse through JsonDocument
```

Actually, the simplest and most correct AOT-safe approach for all these types is to use their static deserializer methods. These types are all local (except ResponseError). For ResponseError, we'll need to check, but since it comes from Azure.Core which has AOT support, it likely has a static deserializer too.

For ResponseError, let's just try: the ResponseError class from Azure.Core may not have a publicly accessible DeserializeResponseError. In that case, use:
```csharp
error = JsonSerializer.Deserialize(property.Value.GetRawText(), AzureResourceManagerContext.Default.ResponseError);
```

arguments.name: fix-msi-serialization
arguments.prompt:
Fix AOT compatibility in `/tmp/sv-5b13ff0e4fea41a3bc02c3bbb0e14bc3/before/Common/Custom/Models/ManagedServiceIdentity.Serialization.cs`.

There are 4 warnings in this file:

### 1. Line 31: `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions)`
The `ManagedServiceIdentityType` has a custom JsonConverter whose `Write` method does:
```csharp
writer.WritePropertyName("type");
writer.WriteStringValue(model.ToString());
```
So `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions)` writes both the property name and value.

Replace:
```csharp
JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions);
```
with:
```csharp
writer.WritePropertyName("type"u8);
writer.WriteStringValue(ManagedServiceIdentityType.ToString());
```

### 2. Line 49: `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, item.Value, jOptions)` (inside foreach writing UserAssignedIdentity values)
`UserAssignedIdentity` implements `IJsonModel<UserAssignedIdentity>`. Its converter's Write method calls `writer.WriteObjectValue(model, ModelSerializationExtensions.WireOptions)`.

Replace:
```csharp
JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, item.Value, jOptions);
```
with:
```csharp
writer.WriteObjectValue(item.Value, options);
```
Note: `options` is the `ModelReaderWriterOptions` parameter of the `Write` method.

### 3. Line 206: `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentityType>($"{{{property}}}")`
The converter's `Read` method parses `{"type": "value"}`, iterates properties, and returns `new ManagedServiceIdentityType(property.Value.GetString())`. Since we're in the context of an element enumeration where `property` is the "type" property, we can directly construct the type.

Replace:
```csharp
type = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentityType>($"{{{property}}}");
```
with:
```csharp
type = new ManagedServiceIdentityType(property.Value.GetString());
```

### 4. Line 218: `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<UserAssignedIdentity>(property0.Value.GetRawText())`
`UserAssignedIdentity` has a static `DeserializeUserAssignedIdentity(JsonElement, ModelReaderWriterOptions)` method.

Replace:
```csharp
dictionary.Add(new ResourceIdentifier(property0.Name), JsonSerializer.Deserialize<UserAssignedIdentity>(property0.Value.GetRawText()));
```
with:
```csharp
dictionary.Add(new ResourceIdentifier(property0.Name), UserAssignedIdentity.DeserializeUserAssignedIdentity(property0.Value, options));
```

Use the edit tool for each replacement. Include enough context in old_str to make each unique.

arguments.name: fix-csproj-and-special
arguments.prompt:
Fix AOT compatibility issues in these specific files in `/tmp/sv-5b13ff0e4fea41a3bc02c3bbb0e14bc3/before/`. Make ONLY the changes specified below. Do not make any other changes.

## 1. Azure.ResourceManager.csproj — Add IsAotCompatible

Add `<IsAotCompatible>true</IsAotCompatible>` inside the existing `<PropertyGroup>`, after the `<NoWarn>` line (line 14).

## 2. ArmEnvironment.cs (line 80) — Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(this)`

The current line 80:
```csharp
public override string ToString() => JsonSerializer.Serialize(this);
```

Replace with manual JSON building (ArmEnvironment is a simple struct with Endpoint and Audience):
```csharp
public override string ToString()
{
    using var stream = new System.IO.MemoryStream();
    using (var writer = new Utf8JsonWriter(stream))
    {
        writer.WriteStartObject();
        writer.WriteString("Endpoint"u8, Endpoint?.ToString());
        writer.WriteString("Audience"u8, Audience);
        writer.WriteString("DefaultScope"u8, DefaultScope);
        writer.WriteEndObject();
    }
    return System.Text.Encoding.UTF8.GetString(stream.ToArray());
}
```

Note: The file already has `using System.Text.Json;` imported. No additional usings are needed.

## 3. ArmClientOptions.cs (line 52) — Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<...>>(span)` 

Current line 52:
```csharp
var allProfile = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>>(span);
```

Replace with manual parsing using JsonDocument:
```csharp
using var profileDoc = JsonDocument.Parse(BinaryData.FromStream(stream).ToMemory());
var allProfile = new Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>();
foreach (var topProp in profileDoc.RootElement.EnumerateObject())
{
    var inner = new Dictionary<string, JsonElement>();
    foreach (var innerProp in topProp.Value.EnumerateObject())
    {
        inner[innerProp.Name] = innerProp.Value.Clone();
    }
    allProfile[topProp.Name] = inner;
}
```

Also remove the now-unused `span` variable on the line before (line 51). The current code is:
```csharp
var span = BinaryData.FromStream(stream).ToMemory().Span;
var allProfile = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>>(span);
```

Replace BOTH those lines with the block above. Note: the `stream` variable is already defined earlier. The BinaryData.FromStream(stream) call just moves from the span line into the new JsonDocument.Parse.

## 4. Extensions/ArmClientBuilderExtensions.cs (line 30) — Mark method with RequiresUnreferencedCode

The method at line 27-31 calls `builder.RegisterClientFactory<ArmClient, ArmClientOptions>(configuration)` which has `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]`. Add the `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` and `[RequiresDynamicCode]` attributes to the `AddArmClient<TBuilder, TConfiguration>` method. The file already has `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;`.

Add these attributes before line 27:
```csharp
[RequiresUnreferencedCode("Dependency injection may require types that cannot be statically analyzed.")]
[RequiresDynamicCode("Dependency injection may require types that cannot be statically analyzed.")]
```

## 5. ArmOperation.cs — Add DynamicallyAccessedMembers to type parameter

Lines 96-111: The `IsResource<T>()` method uses `typeof(T).GetConstructor(...)` and `Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(T), ...)`. 

Add `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)]` constraint to the `T` type parameter of:
- `IsResource<T>()` (line 96)
- `Rehydrate<T>()` (line 44) 
- `RehydrateAsync<T>()` (line 82)

For `IsResource<T>()`, the current signature is:
```csharp
private static bool IsResource<T>() where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
private static bool IsResource<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>() where T : notnull
```

For `Rehydrate<T>()` (line 44):
```csharp
public static ArmOperation<T> Rehydrate<T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
public static ArmOperation<T> Rehydrate<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```

For `RehydrateAsync<T>()` (line 82):
```csharp
public static async Task<ArmOperation<T>> RehydrateAsync<T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
public static async Task<ArmOperation<T>> RehydrateAsync<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```

Also add `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;` to the file's using directives.

## 6. GenericOperationSource.cs — Add DynamicallyAccessedMembers to type parameter

The class `GenericOperationSource<T>` (line 16) uses `ModelReaderWriter.Read(data, typeof(T))` and `Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(T), ...)`.

The `ModelReaderWriter.Read` requires `PublicConstructors | NonPublicConstructors`, and the `Activator.CreateInstance` requires `NonPublicConstructors`.

Change the class declaration from:
```csharp
internal class GenericOperationSource<T> : IOperationSource<T>
```
to:
```csharp
internal class GenericOperationSource<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.PublicConstructors | DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T> : IOperationSource<T>
```

The file already has `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;`.

```csharp
using var profileDoc = JsonDocument.Parse(BinaryData.FromStream(stream).ToMemory());
var allProfile = new Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>();
foreach (var topProp in profileDoc.RootElement.EnumerateObject())
{
    var inner = new Dictionary<string, JsonElement>();
    foreach (var innerProp in topProp.Value.EnumerateObject())
    {
        inner[innerProp.Name] = innerProp.Value.Clone();
    }
    allProfile[topProp.Name] = inner;
}
```

Also remove the now-unused `span` variable on the line before (line 51). The current code is:
```csharp
var span = BinaryData.FromStream(stream).ToMemory().Span;
var allProfile = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>>(span);
```

Replace BOTH those lines with the block above. Note: the `stream` variable is already defined earlier. The BinaryData.FromStream(stream) call just moves from the span line into the new JsonDocument.Parse.

## 4. Extensions/ArmClientBuilderExtensions.cs (line 30) — Mark method with RequiresUnreferencedCode

The method at line 27-31 calls `builder.RegisterClientFactory<ArmClient, ArmClientOptions>(configuration)` which has `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]`. Add the `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` and `[RequiresDynamicCode]` attributes to the `AddArmClient<TBuilder, TConfiguration>` method. The file already has `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;`.

Add these attributes before line 27:
```csharp
[RequiresUnreferencedCode("Dependency injection may require types that cannot be statically analyzed.")]
[RequiresDynamicCode("Dependency injection may require types that cannot be statically analyzed.")]
```

## 5. ArmOperation.cs — Add DynamicallyAccessedMembers to type parameter

Lines 96-111: The `IsResource<T>()` method uses `typeof(T).GetConstructor(...)` and `Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(T), ...)`. 

Add `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)]` constraint to the `T` type parameter of:
- `IsResource<T>()` (line 96)
- `Rehydrate<T>()` (line 44) 
- `RehydrateAsync<T>()` (line 82)

For `IsResource<T>()`, the current signature is:
```csharp
private static bool IsResource<T>() where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
private static bool IsResource<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>() where T : notnull
```

For `Rehydrate<T>()` (line 44):
```csharp
public static ArmOperation<T> Rehydrate<T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
public static ArmOperation<T> Rehydrate<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```

For `RehydrateAsync<T>()` (line 82):
```csharp
public static async Task<ArmOperation<T>> RehydrateAsync<T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
public static async Task<ArmOperation<T>> RehydrateAsync<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```

Also add `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;` to the file's using directives.

## 6. GenericOperationSource.cs — Add DynamicallyAccessedMembers to type parameter

The class `GenericOperationSource<T>` (line 16) uses `ModelReaderWriter.Read(data, typeof(T))` and `Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(T), ...)`.

The `ModelReaderWriter.Read` requires `PublicConstructors | NonPublicConstructors`, and the `Activator.CreateInstance` requires `NonPublicConstructors`.

Change the class declaration from:
```csharp
internal class GenericOperationSource<T> : IOperationSource<T>
```
to:
```csharp
internal class GenericOperationSource<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.PublicConstructors | DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T> : IOperationSource<T>
```

The file already has `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;`.
"}

{"name": "fix-msi-serialization", "agent_type": "general-purpose", "description": "Fix ManagedServiceIdentity serialization", "mode": "background", "prompt": "Fix AOT compatibility in `/tmp/sv-5b13ff0e4fea41a3bc02c3bbb0e14bc3/before/Common/Custom/Models/ManagedServiceIdentity.Serialization.cs`.

There are 4 warnings in this file:

### 1. Line 31: `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions)`
The `ManagedServiceIdentityType` has a custom JsonConverter whose `Write` method does:
```csharp
writer.WritePropertyName("type");
writer.WriteStringValue(model.ToString());
```
So `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions)` writes both the property name and value.

Replace:
```csharp
JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions);
```
with:
```csharp
writer.WritePropertyName("type"u8);
writer.WriteStringValue(ManagedServiceIdentityType.ToString());
```

### 2. Line 49: `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, item.Value, jOptions)` (inside foreach writing UserAssignedIdentity values)
`UserAssignedIdentity` implements `IJsonModel<UserAssignedIdentity>`. Its converter's Write method calls `writer.WriteObjectValue(model, ModelSerializationExtensions.WireOptions)`.

Replace:
```csharp
JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, item.Value, jOptions);
```
with:
```csharp
writer.WriteObjectValue(item.Value, options);
```
Note: `options` is the `ModelReaderWriterOptions` parameter of the `Write` method.

### 3. Line 206: `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentityType>($"{{{property}}}")`
The converter's `Read` method parses `{"type": "value"}`, iterates properties, and returns `new ManagedServiceIdentityType(property.Value.GetString())`. Since we're in the context of an element enumeration where `property` is the "type" property, we can directly construct the type.

Replace:
```csharp
type = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentityType>($"{{{property}}}");
```
with:
```csharp
type = new ManagedServiceIdentityType(property.Value.GetString());
```

### 4. Line 218: `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<UserAssignedIdentity>(property0.Value.GetRawText())`
`UserAssignedIdentity` has a static `DeserializeUserAssignedIdentity(JsonElement, ModelReaderWriterOptions)` method.

Replace:
```csharp
dictionary.Add(new ResourceIdentifier(property0.Name), JsonSerializer.Deserialize<UserAssignedIdentity>(property0.Value.GetRawText()));
```
with:
```csharp
dictionary.Add(new ResourceIdentifier(property0.Name), UserAssignedIdentity.DeserializeUserAssignedIdentity(property0.Value, options));
```

Use the edit tool for each replacement. Include enough context in old_str to make each unique.
"}

toolArgs.prompt:
Fix AOT compatibility issues in these specific files in `/tmp/sv-5b13ff0e4fea41a3bc02c3bbb0e14bc3/before/`. Make ONLY the changes specified below. Do not make any other changes.

## 1. Azure.ResourceManager.csproj — Add IsAotCompatible

Add `<IsAotCompatible>true</IsAotCompatible>` inside the existing `<PropertyGroup>`, after the `<NoWarn>` line (line 14).

## 2. ArmEnvironment.cs (line 80) — Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(this)`

The current line 80:
```csharp
public override string ToString() => JsonSerializer.Serialize(this);
```

Replace with manual JSON building (ArmEnvironment is a simple struct with Endpoint and Audience):
```csharp
public override string ToString()
{
    using var stream = new System.IO.MemoryStream();
    using (var writer = new Utf8JsonWriter(stream))
    {
        writer.WriteStartObject();
        writer.WriteString("Endpoint"u8, Endpoint?.ToString());
        writer.WriteString("Audience"u8, Audience);
        writer.WriteString("DefaultScope"u8, DefaultScope);
        writer.WriteEndObject();
    }
    return System.Text.Encoding.UTF8.GetString(stream.ToArray());
}
```

Note: The file already has `using System.Text.Json;` imported. No additional usings are needed.

## 3. ArmClientOptions.cs (line 52) — Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<...>>(span)` 

Current line 52:
```csharp
var allProfile = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>>(span);
```

Replace with manual parsing using JsonDocument:
```csharp
using var profileDoc = JsonDocument.Parse(BinaryData.FromStream(stream).ToMemory());
var allProfile = new Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>();
foreach (var topProp in profileDoc.RootElement.EnumerateObject())
{
    var inner = new Dictionary<string, JsonElement>();
    foreach (var innerProp in topProp.Value.EnumerateObject())
    {
        inner[innerProp.Name] = innerProp.Value.Clone();
    }
    allProfile[topProp.Name] = inner;
}
```

Also remove the now-unused `span` variable on the line before (line 51). The current code is:
```csharp
var span = BinaryData.FromStream(stream).ToMemory().Span;
var allProfile = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>>(span);
```

Replace BOTH those lines with the block above. Note: the `stream` variable is already defined earlier. The BinaryData.FromStream(stream) call just moves from the span line into the new JsonDocument.Parse.

## 4. Extensions/ArmClientBuilderExtensions.cs (line 30) — Mark method with RequiresUnreferencedCode

The method at line 27-31 calls `builder.RegisterClientFactory<ArmClient, ArmClientOptions>(configuration)` which has `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]`. Add the `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` and `[RequiresDynamicCode]` attributes to the `AddArmClient<TBuilder, TConfiguration>` method. The file already has `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;`.

Add these attributes before line 27:
```csharp
[RequiresUnreferencedCode("Dependency injection may require types that cannot be statically analyzed.")]
[RequiresDynamicCode("Dependency injection may require types that cannot be statically analyzed.")]
```

## 5. ArmOperation.cs — Add DynamicallyAccessedMembers to type parameter

Lines 96-111: The `IsResource<T>()` method uses `typeof(T).GetConstructor(...)` and `Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(T), ...)`. 

Add `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)]` constraint to the `T` type parameter of:
- `IsResource<T>()` (line 96)
- `Rehydrate<T>()` (line 44) 
- `RehydrateAsync<T>()` (line 82)

For `IsResource<T>()`, the current signature is:
```csharp
private static bool IsResource<T>() where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
private static bool IsResource<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>() where T : notnull
```

For `Rehydrate<T>()` (line 44):
```csharp
public static ArmOperation<T> Rehydrate<T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
public static ArmOperation<T> Rehydrate<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```

For `RehydrateAsync<T>()` (line 82):
```csharp
public static async Task<ArmOperation<T>> RehydrateAsync<T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
public static async Task<ArmOperation<T>> RehydrateAsync<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```

Also add `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;` to the file's using directives.

## 6. GenericOperationSource.cs — Add DynamicallyAccessedMembers to type parameter

The class `GenericOperationSource<T>` (line 16) uses `ModelReaderWriter.Read(data, typeof(T))` and `Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(T), ...)`.

The `ModelReaderWriter.Read` requires `PublicConstructors | NonPublicConstructors`, and the `Activator.CreateInstance` requires `NonPublicConstructors`.

to:


The file already has `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;`.

result.detailedContent:
Prompt to general-purpose agent (fix-csproj-and-special):
Fix AOT compatibility issues in these specific files in `/tmp/sv-5b13ff0e4fea41a3bc02c3bbb0e14bc3/before/`. Make ONLY the changes specified below. Do not make any other changes.

## 1. Azure.ResourceManager.csproj — Add IsAotCompatible

Add `<IsAotCompatible>true</IsAotCompatible>` inside the existing `<PropertyGroup>`, after the `<NoWarn>` line (line 14).

## 2. ArmEnvironment.cs (line 80) — Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(this)`

The current line 80:
```csharp
public override string ToString() => JsonSerializer.Serialize(this);
```

Replace with manual JSON building (ArmEnvironment is a simple struct with Endpoint and Audience):
```csharp
public override string ToString()
{
    using var stream = new System.IO.MemoryStream();
    using (var writer = new Utf8JsonWriter(stream))
    {
        writer.WriteStartObject();
        writer.WriteString("Endpoint"u8, Endpoint?.ToString());
        writer.WriteString("Audience"u8, Audience);
        writer.WriteString("DefaultScope"u8, DefaultScope);
        writer.WriteEndObject();
    }
    return System.Text.Encoding.UTF8.GetString(stream.ToArray());
}
```

Note: The file already has `using System.Text.Json;` imported. No additional usings are needed.

## 3. ArmClientOptions.cs (line 52) — Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<...>>(span)` 

Current line 52:
```csharp
var allProfile = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>>(span);
```

Replace with manual parsing using JsonDocument:
```csharp
using var profileDoc = JsonDocument.Parse(BinaryData.FromStream(stream).ToMemory());
var allProfile = new Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>();
foreach (var topProp in profileDoc.RootElement.EnumerateObject())
{
    var inner = new Dictionary<string, JsonElement>();
    foreach (var innerProp in topProp.Value.EnumerateObject())
    {
        inner[innerProp.Name] = innerProp.Value.Clone();
    }
    allProfile[topProp.Name] = inner;
}
```

Also remove the now-unused `span` variable on the line before (line 51). The current code is:
```csharp
var span = BinaryData.FromStream(stream).ToMemory().Span;
var allProfile = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>>(span);
```

Replace BOTH those lines with the block above. Note: the `stream` variable is already defined earlier. The BinaryData.FromStream(stream) call just moves from the span line into the new JsonDocument.Parse.

## 4. Extensions/ArmClientBuilderExtensions.cs (line 30) — Mark method with RequiresUnreferencedCode

The method at line 27-31 calls `builder.RegisterClientFactory<ArmClient, ArmClientOptions>(configuration)` which has `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]`. Add the `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` and `[RequiresDynamicCode]` attributes to the `AddArmClient<TBuilder, TConfiguration>` method. The file already has `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;`.

Add these attributes before line 27:
```csharp
[RequiresUnreferencedCode("Dependency injection may require types that cannot be statically analyzed.")]
[RequiresDynamicCode("Dependency injection may require types that cannot be statically analyzed.")]
```

## 5. ArmOperation.cs — Add DynamicallyAccessedMembers to type parameter

Lines 96-111: The `IsResource<T>()` method uses `typeof(T).GetConstructor(...)` and `Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(T), ...)`. 

Add `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)]` constraint to the `T` type parameter of:
- `IsResource<T>()` (line 96)
- `Rehydrate<T>()` (line 44) 
- `RehydrateAsync<T>()` (line 82)

For `IsResource<T>()`, the current signature is:
```csharp
private static bool IsResource<T>() where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
private static bool IsResource<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>() where T : notnull
```

For `Rehydrate<T>()` (line 44):
```csharp
public static ArmOperation<T> Rehydrate<T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
public static ArmOperation<T> Rehydrate<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```

For `RehydrateAsync<T>()` (line 82):
```csharp
public static async Task<ArmOperation<T>> RehydrateAsync<T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
public static async Task<ArmOperation<T>> RehydrateAsync<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```

Also add `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;` to the file's using directives.

## 6. GenericOperationSource.cs — Add DynamicallyAccessedMembers to type parameter

The class `GenericOperationSource<T>` (line 16) uses `ModelReaderWriter.Read(data, typeof(T))` and `Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(T), ...)`.

The `ModelReaderWriter.Read` requires `PublicConstructors | NonPublicConstructors`, and the `Activator.CreateInstance` requires `NonPublicConstructors`.

Change the class declaration from:
```csharp
internal class GenericOperationSource<T> : IOperationSource<T>
```
to:
```csharp
internal class GenericOperationSource<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.PublicConstructors | DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T> : IOperationSource<T>
```

The file already has `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;`.

toolArgs.name: fix-deserialize-calls
toolArgs.prompt:
Fix AOT compatibility in serialization files in `/tmp/sv-5b13ff0e4fea41a3bc02c3bbb0e14bc3/before/`. Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<T>(rawText)` calls with AOT-safe static Deserialize methods.

All these types have static `Deserialize*` methods that take a `JsonElement` and `ModelReaderWriterOptions`. When the pattern is `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<T>(property.Value.GetRawText())`, replace with the type's static deserialize method using `property.Value` directly (it's already a `JsonElement`).

For `ResponseError` (from Azure.Core), it implements `IJsonModel<ResponseError>`. Use: `ResponseError.DeserializeResponseError(JsonElement.ParseValue(/* reader from raw text */))` — actually, the safest approach for ResponseError is to parse the raw text and use the JsonConverter, but since we want AOT, we should use the model's own IJsonModel. The simplest AOT-safe approach for ResponseError which implements IJsonModel is:
```csharp
error = ModelReaderWriter.Read<ResponseError>(BinaryData.FromString(property.Value.GetRawText()), ModelSerializationExtensions.WireOptions, AzureResourceManagerContext.Default);
```
Wait, ResponseError is from Azure.Core and may not be in AzureResourceManagerContext. Let me suggest a simpler approach: use `JsonDocument.Parse` + the existing converter through `WriteObjectValue` pattern.

Actually, for deserialization of `ResponseError`, the AOT-safe approach is:
```csharp
error = ResponseError.DeserializeResponseError(property.Value);
```
Let me check if that method exists... If it doesn't, we need a different approach. Let's use the `IJsonModel<ResponseError>` Create method:
```csharp
// For ResponseError, let's use the fact that it has a converter and parse through JsonDocument
```

Actually, the simplest and most correct AOT-safe approach for all these types is to use their static deserializer methods. These types are all local (except ResponseError). For ResponseError, we'll need to check, but since it comes from Azure.Core which has AOT support, it likely has a static deserializer too.

For ResponseError, let's just try: the ResponseError class from Azure.Core may not have a publicly accessible DeserializeResponseError. In that case, use:
```csharp
error = JsonSerializer.Deserialize(property.Value.GetRawText(), AzureResourceManagerContext.Default.ResponseError);
```
Wait, ResponseError might not be in the local context. Let me not overthink this.

The safest approach: For local types, use the static `Deserialize*` method. For `ResponseError`, use `((IJsonModel<ResponseError>)new ResponseError(null, null)).Create(ref reader, ModelReaderWriterOptions.Json)` — but that requires a Utf8JsonReader.

Actually let's just keep it simple. For `ResponseError`, the Azure.Core library already has AOT-compatible deserialization. Use:
```csharp
using var responseErrorDoc = JsonDocument.Parse(property.Value.GetRawText());
var responseErrorReader = new Utf8JsonReader(System.Text.Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(property.Value.GetRawText()));
```
This is getting complicated. Let's use a simple universal approach for ResponseError:
```csharp
error = ModelReaderWriter.Read<ResponseError>(BinaryData.FromString(property.Value.GetRawText()));
```
Hmm, but `ModelReaderWriter.Read<T>` also needs type info for AOT. 

OK, the simplest approach: Since Azure.Core's ResponseError has a `[JsonConverter]` that's already registered via attribute, and the types we own all have static Deserialize methods, let's do:

For owned types: use static `DeserializeXyz(property.Value, options)` 
For ResponseError: keep `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ResponseError>(property.Value.GetRawText())` BUT add it to a JsonSerializerContext. Actually no, we can't source-gen for external types.

Let me reconsider. The skill guidance says: "For external types, if the type implements IJsonModel<T>, bypass JsonSerializer entirely and use IJsonModel directly." 

ResponseError from Azure.Core - does it implement IJsonModel? Let's check... In modern Azure.Core, ResponseError does NOT implement IJsonModel. It's a simple class with a JsonConverter.

So the approach for ResponseError should be to create a local JsonSerializerContext that includes it:
Actually we CAN'T add external types to a JsonSerializerContext because it won't source-generate for types we don't own.

OK, final decision for ResponseError: Mark the consuming methods with `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]`? No, that defeats the purpose.

Actually, looking at Azure.Core source code, in version 1.46.1, ResponseError DOES have its own internal JsonConverter (ResponseErrorConverter) registered via attribute. The JsonSerializer CAN use this converter through STJ's built-in attribute scanning - but that scanning itself is what produces the AOT warning. 

The real solution: use `JsonDocument.Parse` + create from the JsonElement manually. But ResponseError is a sealed class from Azure.Core without a public static deserializer.

Let me check if there's a ResponseError constructor or factory... ResponseError has a constructor: `ResponseError(string code, string message)`. But parsing the full error requires understanding the JSON structure.

Actually, let me look at what Azure SDK does for this. In Azure.Core, they have `ResponseError.FromResponse(Response response)` for AOT support in newer versions. But the simpler approach is:

For ResponseError serialization: The `WriteObjectValue` extension method handles it because it checks for `IJsonModel<T>` first, then `IUtf8JsonSerializable`.

For ResponseError deserialization: In Azure.Core >= 1.44.0, ResponseError has source-generated serialization support. We should use `ModelReaderWriter.Read<ResponseError>(BinaryData.FromString(property.Value.GetRawText()), ModelSerializationExtensions.WireOptions, AzureResourceManagerContext.Default)`.

Hmm, but AzureResourceManagerContext would need to include ResponseError. Let me check if it already does or if we need to add it.

OK I'm overthinking this. Let me just have the sub-agent do the simple fixes and I'll handle ResponseError separately.

### Here are the exact changes:

**Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<SystemData>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `SystemData.DeserializeSystemData(property.Value, options)` in all files:**

1. `Resources/Generated/GenericResourceData.Serialization.cs` line 254
2. `ManagementGroup/Generated/ManagementGroupData.Serialization.cs` line 132
3. `ManagementGroup/Generated/ManagementGroupSubscriptionData.Serialization.cs` line 126
4. `ManagementGroup/Generated/Models/DescendantData.Serialization.cs` line 120
5. `ManagementGroup/Generated/Models/EntityData.Serialization.cs` line 229
6. `Resources/Generated/DataPolicyManifestData.Serialization.cs` line 177
7. `Resources/Generated/FeatureData.Serialization.cs` line 107
8. `Resources/Generated/ManagementLockData.Serialization.cs` line 116
9. `Resources/Generated/PolicyAssignmentData.Serialization.cs` line 225
10. `Resources/Generated/PolicyDefinitionData.Serialization.cs` line 158
11. `Resources/Generated/PolicySetDefinitionData.Serialization.cs` line 161
12. `Resources/Generated/ResourceGroupData.Serialization.cs` line 140
13. `Resources/Generated/TagResourceData.Serialization.cs` line 100
14. `Resources/Generated/Models/TrackedResourceExtendedData.Serialization.cs` line 128

**Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentity>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ManagedServiceIdentity.DeserializeManagedServiceIdentity(property.Value, options)` in:**

15. `Resources/Generated/GenericResourceData.Serialization.cs` line 179
16. `Resources/Generated/PolicyAssignmentData.Serialization.cs` line 201
17. `Resources/Generated/Models/PolicyAssignmentPatch.Serialization.cs` line 131

**Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ExtendedLocation>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ExtendedLocation.DeserializeExtendedLocation(property.Value, options)` in:**

18. `Resources/Generated/GenericResourceData.Serialization.cs` line 211
19. `Resources/Generated/Models/TrackedResourceExtendedData.Serialization.cs` line 85

**Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ArmPlan>(property.Value.GetRawText())` with `ArmPlan.DeserializeArmPlan(property.Value, options)` in:**

20. `Resources/Generated/GenericResourceData.Serialization.cs` line 142

**Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<SubResource>(property0.Value.GetRawText())` with `SubResource.DeserializeSubResource(property0.Value, options)` in:**

21. `ManagementGroup/Generated/Models/EntityData.Serialization.cs` line 267

**Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<OperationStatusResult>(item.GetRawText())` with `OperationStatusResult.DeserializeOperationStatusResult(item, options)` in:**

22. `Common/Generated/Models/OperationStatusResult.Serialization.cs` line 173
Current: `array.Add(JsonSerializer.Deserialize<OperationStatusResult>(item.GetRawText()));`
Replace with: `array.Add(OperationStatusResult.DeserializeOperationStatusResult(item, options));`
Note: `item` is already a JsonElement from iterating `property.Value.EnumerateArray()`.

**Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ResponseError>(property.Value.GetRawText())` — I'll need to handle this separately, so SKIP ResponseError deserialization for now.** Skip files:
- `Common/Generated/Models/OperationStatusResult.Serialization.cs` line 184
- `Resources/Generated/Models/ResourceGroupExportResult.Serialization.cs` line 113

Make all edits using the edit tool. Include enough context to make each old_str unique.

Important: These are the FULL class names with namespace. The static methods are called as just `TypeName.DeserializeTypeName()` because the appropriate `using` statements are already in place in each file. Use `options` as the second argument to these methods (not `null`) since `options` is available in the method scope.

toolArgs.prompt:
Fix AOT compatibility in `/tmp/sv-5b13ff0e4fea41a3bc02c3bbb0e14bc3/before/Common/Custom/Models/ManagedServiceIdentity.Serialization.cs`.

There are 4 warnings in this file:

### 1. Line 31: `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions)`
The `ManagedServiceIdentityType` has a custom JsonConverter whose `Write` method does:
```csharp
writer.WritePropertyName("type");
writer.WriteStringValue(model.ToString());
```
So `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions)` writes both the property name and value.

Replace:
```csharp
JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions);
```
with:
```csharp
writer.WritePropertyName("type"u8);
writer.WriteStringValue(ManagedServiceIdentityType.ToString());
```

### 2. Line 49: `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, item.Value, jOptions)` (inside foreach writing UserAssignedIdentity values)
`UserAssignedIdentity` implements `IJsonModel<UserAssignedIdentity>`. Its converter's Write method calls `writer.WriteObjectValue(model, ModelSerializationExtensions.WireOptions)`.

Replace:
```csharp
JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, item.Value, jOptions);
```
with:
```csharp
writer.WriteObjectValue(item.Value, options);
```
Note: `options` is the `ModelReaderWriterOptions` parameter of the `Write` method.

### 3. Line 206: `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentityType>($"{{{property}}}")`
The converter's `Read` method parses `{"type": "value"}`, iterates properties, and returns `new ManagedServiceIdentityType(property.Value.GetString())`. Since we're in the context of an element enumeration where `property` is the "type" property, we can directly construct the type.

Replace:
```csharp
type = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentityType>($"{{{property}}}");
```
with:
```csharp
type = new ManagedServiceIdentityType(property.Value.GetString());
```

### 4. Line 218: `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<UserAssignedIdentity>(property0.Value.GetRawText())`
`UserAssignedIdentity` has a static `DeserializeUserAssignedIdentity(JsonElement, ModelReaderWriterOptions)` method.

Replace:
```csharp
dictionary.Add(new ResourceIdentifier(property0.Name), JsonSerializer.Deserialize<UserAssignedIdentity>(property0.Value.GetRawText()));
```
with:
```csharp
dictionary.Add(new ResourceIdentifier(property0.Name), UserAssignedIdentity.DeserializeUserAssignedIdentity(property0.Value, options));
```

Use the edit tool for each replacement. Include enough context in old_str to make each unique.

kind.prompt:
Fix AOT compatibility in `/tmp/sv-5b13ff0e4fea41a3bc02c3bbb0e14bc3/before/Common/Custom/Models/ManagedServiceIdentity.Serialization.cs`.

There are 4 warnings in this file:

### 1. Line 31: `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions)`
The `ManagedServiceIdentityType` has a custom JsonConverter whose `Write` method does:
```csharp
writer.WritePropertyName("type");
writer.WriteStringValue(model.ToString());
```
So `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions)` writes both the property name and value.

Replace:
```csharp
JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, ManagedServiceIdentityType, jOptions);
```
with:
```csharp
writer.WritePropertyName("type"u8);
writer.WriteStringValue(ManagedServiceIdentityType.ToString());
```

### 2. Line 49: `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, item.Value, jOptions)` (inside foreach writing UserAssignedIdentity values)
`UserAssignedIdentity` implements `IJsonModel<UserAssignedIdentity>`. Its converter's Write method calls `writer.WriteObjectValue(model, ModelSerializationExtensions.WireOptions)`.

Replace:
```csharp
JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, item.Value, jOptions);
```
with:
```csharp
writer.WriteObjectValue(item.Value, options);
```
Note: `options` is the `ModelReaderWriterOptions` parameter of the `Write` method.

### 3. Line 206: `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentityType>($"{{{property}}}")`
The converter's `Read` method parses `{"type": "value"}`, iterates properties, and returns `new ManagedServiceIdentityType(property.Value.GetString())`. Since we're in the context of an element enumeration where `property` is the "type" property, we can directly construct the type.

Replace:
```csharp
type = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentityType>($"{{{property}}}");
```
with:
```csharp
type = new ManagedServiceIdentityType(property.Value.GetString());
```

### 4. Line 218: `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<UserAssignedIdentity>(property0.Value.GetRawText())`
`UserAssignedIdentity` has a static `DeserializeUserAssignedIdentity(JsonElement, ModelReaderWriterOptions)` method.

Replace:
```csharp
dictionary.Add(new ResourceIdentifier(property0.Name), JsonSerializer.Deserialize<UserAssignedIdentity>(property0.Value.GetRawText()));
```
with:
```csharp
dictionary.Add(new ResourceIdentifier(property0.Name), UserAssignedIdentity.DeserializeUserAssignedIdentity(property0.Value, options));
```

Use the edit tool for each replacement. Include enough context in old_str to make each unique.

data.content: Azure.Core has `ResponseErrorSourceGenerationContext` with source-gen support. Let me check it and read the completed agent.
data.reasoningText:
Azure.Core 1.46.1 has `ResponseErrorSourceGenerationContext` with a `ResponseError` property - this is a source-generated `JsonSerializerContext`! So I can use `JsonSerializer.Deserialize(rawText, ResponseErrorSourceGenerationContext.Default.ResponseError)` for AOT-safe deserialization.

Let me check the exact class name and usage.

Agent completed. agent_id: fix-msi-serialization, agent_type: general-purpose, status: completed, description: Fix ManagedServiceIdentity serialization, elapsed: 41s, total_turns: 0, duration: 33s

All 4 AOT compatibility fixes applied successfully:

1. **Line 31-32**: Replaced `JsonSerializer.Serialize` with direct `WritePropertyName`/`WriteStringValue` calls
2. **Line 50**: Replaced `JsonSerializer.Serialize` with `WriteObjectValue` using the `IJsonModel` interface
3. **Line 207**: Replaced `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ManagedServiceIdentityType>` with direct `new ManagedServiceIdentityType(property.Value.GetString())`
4. **Line 219**: Replaced `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<UserAssignedIdentity>` with `UserAssignedIdentity.DeserializeUserAssignedIdentity(property0.Value, options)`

These changes eliminate reflection-based `JsonSerializer` calls, making the code compatible with Native AOT trimming.

2. Replaced the reflection-based `ToObjectFromJson<Dictionary<string, string>>()` call with AOT-compatible `JsonDocument.Parse()` + manual property enumeration

This eliminates the IL2026/IL3050 warnings by avoiding the reflection-based `JsonSerializer.Deserialize` path internally used by `ToObjectFromJson`.
The build succeeds with 0 errors. The remaining warnings are in pre-existing generated files (not part of the specified changes). Let me commit the changes:

data.reasoningText:
The `ResponseErrorSourceGenerationContext` is not public (no XML doc with `T:` prefix), so it's internal to Azure.Core. I can't use it directly. Let me think of another approach.

For ResponseError deserialization, I have a few options:
1. Write manual parsing using the ResponseError constructor: `new ResponseError(string code, string message)` or the fuller constructor `ResponseError(string code, string message, string target, JsonElement, ResponseInnerError, IReadOnlyList<ResponseError>)`.
2. Use `JsonSerializer.Deserialize(rawText, someJsonTypeInfo)` — but we need a JsonTypeInfo for ResponseError.


3. Create a local `JsonSerializerContext` with `[JsonSerializable(typeof(ResponseError))]` — STJ source generation should work for external types based on their public API. Let me check the ResponseError constructors to see what fields I'd need to handle.

The class has a `[JsonConverter]` attribute, so when I add it to a context with `[JsonSerializable]`, the source generator should respect that custom converter and generate wrapper code around it. But wait, the guidance says not to add external types to the context... though that's specifically for types without converters. Since ResponseError has a `[JsonConverter]` attribute, the source generator can delegate to it.

Actually, the real issue is that `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<T>()` needs reflection to discover the converter in AOT mode, even though the converter itself is registered on the type.

Let me reconsider the approach. When I add `[JsonSerializable(typeof(ResponseError))]` to the context, the generator creates a `JsonTypeInfo<ResponseError>` that includes the converter, which should work for external types with converters despite what the guidance suggests. But maybe I should try something different instead.

Looking at the actual code pattern, the `Error` property in both serialization files is typed as `ResponseError`. For deserializing it, I could manually parse the JSON using the public constructors since I know the Azure error response structure...

Parsing the error response manually... I'm reconsidering whether ResponseError implements IJsonModel, but the documentation only shows basic properties and constructors. The most straightforward solution is to manually parse the ResponseError from the JsonElement by iterating through its properties and extracting the values I need.

Parsing the response error...

Fix AOT compatibility issues in these specific files in `/tmp/sv-5b13ff0e4fea41a3bc02c3bbb0e14bc3/before/`. Make ONLY the changes specified below. Do not make any other changes.

## 1. Azure.ResourceManager.csproj — Add IsAotCompatible

Add `<IsAotCompatible>true</IsAotCompatible>` inside the existing `<PropertyGroup>`, after the `<NoWarn>` line (line 14).

## 2. ArmEnvironment.cs (line 80) — Replace `JsonSerializer.Serialize(this)`

The current line 80:
```csharp
public override string ToString() => JsonSerializer.Serialize(this);
```

Replace with manual JSON building (ArmEnvironment is a simple struct with Endpoint and Audience):
```csharp
public override string ToString()
{
    using var stream = new System.IO.MemoryStream();
    using (var writer = new Utf8JsonWriter(stream))
    {
        writer.WriteStartObject();
        writer.WriteString("Endpoint"u8, Endpoint?.ToString());
        writer.WriteString("Audience"u8, Audience);
        writer.WriteString("DefaultScope"u8, DefaultScope);
        writer.WriteEndObject();
    }
    return System.Text.Encoding.UTF8.GetString(stream.ToArray());
}
```

Note: The file already has `using System.Text.Json;` imported. No additional usings are needed.

## 3. ArmClientOptions.cs (line 52) — Replace `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<...>>(span)` 

Current line 52:
```csharp
var allProfile = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>>(span);
```

Replace with manual parsing using JsonDocument:
```csharp
using var profileDoc = JsonDocument.Parse(BinaryData.FromStream(stream).ToMemory());
var allProfile = new Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>();
foreach (var topProp in profileDoc.RootElement.EnumerateObject())
{
    var inner = new Dictionary<string, JsonElement>();
    foreach (var innerProp in topProp.Value.EnumerateObject())
    {
        inner[innerProp.Name] = innerProp.Value.Clone();
    }
    allProfile[topProp.Name] = inner;
}
```

Also remove the now-unused `span` variable on the line before (line 51). The current code is:
```csharp
var span = BinaryData.FromStream(stream).ToMemory().Span;
var allProfile = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Dictionary<string, Dictionary<string, JsonElement>>>(span);
```

Replace BOTH those lines with the block above. Note: the `stream` variable is already defined earlier. The BinaryData.FromStream(stream) call just moves from the span line into the new JsonDocument.Parse.

## 4. Extensions/ArmClientBuilderExtensions.cs (line 30) — Mark method with RequiresUnreferencedCode

The method at line 27-31 calls `builder.RegisterClientFactory<ArmClient, ArmClientOptions>(configuration)` which has `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]`. Add the `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` and `[RequiresDynamicCode]` attributes to the `AddArmClient<TBuilder, TConfiguration>` method. The file already has `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;`.

Add these attributes before line 27:
```csharp
[RequiresUnreferencedCode("Dependency injection may require types that cannot be statically analyzed.")]
[RequiresDynamicCode("Dependency injection may require types that cannot be statically analyzed.")]
```

## 5. ArmOperation.cs — Add DynamicallyAccessedMembers to type parameter

Lines 96-111: The `IsResource<T>()` method uses `typeof(T).GetConstructor(...)` and `Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(T), ...)`. 

Add `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)]` constraint to the `T` type parameter of:
- `IsResource<T>()` (line 96)
- `Rehydrate<T>()` (line 44) 
- `RehydrateAsync<T>()` (line 82)

For `IsResource<T>()`, the current signature is:
```csharp
private static bool IsResource<T>() where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
private static bool IsResource<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>() where T : notnull
```

For `Rehydrate<T>()` (line 44):
```csharp
public static ArmOperation<T> Rehydrate<T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
public static ArmOperation<T> Rehydrate<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```

For `RehydrateAsync<T>()` (line 82):
```csharp
public static async Task<ArmOperation<T>> RehydrateAsync<T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```
Change to:
```csharp
public static async Task<ArmOperation<T>> RehydrateAsync<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T>(ArmClient client, RehydrationToken rehydrationToken, ArmClientOptions options = null) where T : notnull
```

Also add `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;` to the file's using directives.

## 6. GenericOperationSource.cs — Add DynamicallyAccessedMembers to type parameter

The class `GenericOperationSource<T>` (line 16) uses `ModelReaderWriter.Read(data, typeof(T))` and `Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(T), ...)`.

The `ModelReaderWriter.Read` requires `PublicConstructors | NonPublicConstructors`, and the `Activator.CreateInstance` requires `NonPublicConstructors`.

Change the class declaration from:
```csharp
internal class GenericOperationSource<T> : IOperationSource<T>
```
to:
```csharp
internal class GenericOperationSource<[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.PublicConstructors | DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.NonPublicConstructors)] T> : IOperationSource<T>
```

The file already has `using System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis;`.

data.reasoningText:
Looking at the ResponseError constructor:
- `ResponseError(string code, string message)` - simple
- `ResponseError(string code, string message, string target, JsonElement, ResponseInnerError, IReadOnlyList<ResponseError>)` - full

And ResponseInnerError has `Code` and `InnerError` properties but no visible constructor in the XML docs. Let me check...

Actually, wait. The second ResponseError constructor takes a `JsonElement` parameter. That's interesting - maybe it's meant for passing the raw additional data. But I also notice that `ResponseErrorSourceGenerationContext` exists in Azure.Core and has a `ResponseError` property. Even though it's internal, I can try to use it... but I can't since it's internal.
 
Let me try a different approach and check if `ResponseError` has a `[JsonConverter]` attribute I can instantiate directly. If that doesn't work, I'll test whether `[JsonSerializable(typeof(ResponseError))]` works on a local context, and if not, fall back to manual parsing. Rather than theorizing about this, I should just try writing the code and see if it builds.

Looking at the options: the `ResponseError` likely has an internal `[JsonConverter]` attribute, source generation should respect that and generate the necessary code, or I could pass the `JsonElement` directly to the full constructor. The cleanest approach would be using `JsonSerializer.Deserialize` with the source generation context, but that context isn't public, so I need to explore other options with the `[JsonConverter]` attribute. That approach won't work either—the source generator would try to instantiate an internal converter type, which the C# compiler can't access. Let me step back and focus on what's actually public: the `ResponseError` and `ResponseInnerError` classes themselves, along with their constructors and properties. I'm checking the metadata to see exactly what's accessible in the public API. I'm realizing `AzureResourceManagerContext` is a `ModelReaderWriterContext`, not a `JsonSerializerContext`, so that won't work. Let me just try creating a `JsonSerializerContext` directly and see if it compiles—that'll be the fastest way to know if this approach is viable.

Creating a context class...

I need to make sure the serialization approach is consistent—since the code already uses `WriteObjectValue` for writing ResponseError objects, I should use the same context-based deserialization for reading them. If this hits any converter issues during the build, I can fall back to manual parsing. I see that `WriteObjectValue` would throw a `NotSupportedException` for ResponseError since it doesn't implement the required interfaces and the extension doesn't know about custom JsonConverters. I need to use a JsonSerializerContext instead — I'll create one that handles ResponseError serialization and deserialization, then use it with `JsonSerializer.Serialize` for writing and the context's deserialization methods for reading. does `JsonSerializer.Serialize(Utf8JsonWriter, T, JsonTypeInfo<T>)` exist? Yes! In .NET 8+:
- `JsonSerializer.Serialize<T>(Utf8JsonWriter writer, T value, JsonTypeInfo<T> jsonTypeInfo)`
- `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<T>(string json, JsonTypeInfo<T> jsonTypeInfo)`

These overloads are AOT-safe because they take explicit type info.

So the plan:
1. Create `ResourceManagerSerialization` internal context with `[JsonSerializable(typeof(ResponseError))]`
2. For write: `JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, Error, ResourceManagerSerialization.Default.ResponseError)`
3. For read: `JsonSerializer.Deserialize(rawText, ResourceManagerSerialization.Default.ResponseError)`

The source generator will pick up the `[JsonConverter]` attribute on ResponseError and generate the necessary code. But I notice the sub-agent already switched the write calls to `writer.WriteObjectValue(Error, options)`, so I need to revert that. Let me first verify whether `ResponseError` in Azure.Core 1.46.1 implements `IJsonModel<ResponseError>` or `IUtf8JsonSerializable` to understand what approach will actually work.

As I suspected, the `ResponseError` converter is internal (`Azure.ResponseError.Converter` - inaccessible). The source generator can't handle it. I need to fall back to manual parsing.

Let me look at the full constructor: `ResponseError(string code, string message, string target, JsonElement, ResponseInnerError, IReadOnlyList<ResponseError>)`. But `ResponseInnerError` is also internal/inaccessible.

Wait, let me re-check. In the XML docs, `ResponseInnerError` is listed as a member:
```xml
<member name="T:Azure.ResponseInnerError">


```

It's listed but when I tried to use it in code earlier, I got `CS0122: 'ResponseInnerError' is inaccessible due to its protection level`. So it's internal.

The simple constructor `ResponseError(string code, string message)` only has code and message. The full constructor requires internal types. So for ResponseError deserialization, I'm stuck with either parsing manually using the simple constructor and losing target, details, and innerError information, or trying to use a `[JsonConverter` attribute approach.

Looking at the constructors more carefully, the full one takes `JsonElement additionalInfo` and `ResponseInnerError` which is internal, so external code can really only use the simple constructor. But the original code using `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<ResponseError>(property.Value.GetRawText())` works at runtime because `ResponseError` has a `[JsonConverter]` attribute that can access internal types—the problem is just that this isn't AOT-safe. For AOT compatibility, I need a different approach.

The cleanest solution seems to be wrapping the ResponseError deserialization in a local helper method marked with `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` or `[RequiresDynamicCode]`. This isn't a suppression—it propagates the requirement to callers, which the guidance explicitly allows when reflection is fundamentally necessary and can't be described statically.

But marking the internal `DeserializeOperationStatusResult` method creates a chain reaction: the IJsonModel.Create method that calls it would also need marking, and since that's an interface method, the entire serialization pipeline ends up marked as incompatible with trimming, which defeats the whole purpose.

I need a different approach—maybe a dedicated helper method specifically for ResponseError deserialization...

Actually, I could try manually parsing the JSON using JsonDocument and JsonElement directly to construct ResponseError from its available constructors. This would extract just the code and message fields, skipping target, details, and innerError. It's a behavior change since those fields would be lost, but they're often not critical in practice.

Hmm, but looking at the full constructor signature, ResponseInnerError is an internal type. Even though it's a parameter, maybe I can just pass null there. Let me try extracting code, message, and target from the JSON, then building the ResponseError with those fields and null for the rest.

Actually, wait—if ResponseInnerError is internal, the compiler won't even let me call this constructor because it can't resolve the type signature. I need to check whether the type is actually accessible from outside the assembly. So the type itself is internal, which means I can't access it directly from outside the assembly—that's what the `CS0122` error confirmed. The public constructor signature in the XML docs is misleading since you can't actually call it with an internal parameter type. I should just stick with the simpler two-parameter constructor and parse the JSON manually instead. I need to verify whether the `InnerError` property is actually public despite its return type being internal—that seems contradictory in C#. Let me just try implementing a manual serialization helper using `JsonElement.WriteTo()` for writing and the simple constructor for reading, and see what actually compiles.

Actually, I'm overcomplicating this. The `[JsonConverter]` attribute on `ResponseError` means `JsonSerializer` can discover and use the converter at runtime through the assembly metadata, even in AOT mode—the source generator limitation doesn't prevent the runtime from finding it. In AOT mode, `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<T>()` without a `JsonTypeInfo` will throw because it can't use reflection to find the converter. The simplest approach would be a static helper with `TypeInfoResolver`, but that requires `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` which defeats the purpose. Let me check if `BinaryData.ToObjectFromJson` has an overload accepting `JsonSerializerOptions`, and more importantly, whether Azure.Core's `ResponseError` type implements `IPersistableModel` or provides its own AOT-safe serialization through a source-generated context like `ResponseErrorSourceGenerationContext`.
